Also, quick tip: don't feel comfortable yet if once you've finished the shown testcases, as you might get deduction points in those hidden ones. This is extremely crucial especially for PAs, as each of them worth 8% of ur final grade

For midterm and final, I'd recommend go revise anything learnt in class, and do all the past papers, and well... on paper, just like the exams :D

Idk tbh imo forcing myself w/ to practice on paper makes me comfortable for writing the strict syntax stuff for midterm and final, as WHY AND WHO DOES THAT ON PAPER

It also deepens my knowledge and intuition, so I'd recommend paper for sure, even though it's kinda like double the effort as u might want to do it in ur coding environment to make sure ur code runs

However, final remarks, the more u put effort the better grade u'll get

comment_content:
The following are the main topics covered in this course:
1- C++ Basics (intro to coding, c++ syntax, types, variables, etc)
2- Control Flow (if statements, while and for loops)
3- Functions (pass-by-value/reference, Recursion, etc)
4- Array (1D and 2D, C-string)
5- Scope
6- Struct
7- Pointer (spent the most time on this topic, l/rvalue, address, "pass-by-pointer", dynamic memory, linked lists, etc)
8- Class
9- Stacks and Queues

Overall, I would consider these topics to be easy and quite basic, as I've already learned about many of them.

Some topics that are more confusing, especially if you have less coding experience, are Recursions and Pointers.

With recursions you have to change the way you think about problems, so I'd suggest spending a bit more time on this topic and do some practice problems and code to get familiar with it. I have learned about recursions before, so I did not struggle much on it.

Pointer is a new concept for me, so I had to spend a bit more effort understanding it, but overall I think it's very simple conceptually. The difficult part is being careful with unusual cases, such as const pointer vs pointer to const and pointers to pointers, and all the cases with linked lists such as the head and tail.

Due to the nature of coding, I'd highly recommend messing around with code on your own. The lecture slides provide lots of example code that you can copy and experiment on. It's useful in helping you build up an instinctive understanding of the C++ syntax and to think like a computer.
comment_teaching:
Dimitris Papadopoulous is a great teacher. I love the way he teaches, especially with how he encourages us to ask questions. He is very engaging and fun to listen to. He also asks good questions that are not on the lecture material that get my head scratching and help me understand the material deeper.

The lecture slides are well made and contain everything you need to know for the course (plus some optional extra info). You do not need to attend lectures to succeed in this course, but in my case I liked Dimitris's teaching so I joined most of them. Like I mentioned in the content section, there are also lots of example code that I suggest you copy and practice with.

Overall, the course content is structured clearly and helpful in understanding. You can do well on the exams just by studying the lecture slides and past-papers.
comment_workload:
I have previously learned Java and self learned Python, so I was able to understand most of the course's topic without spending any time studying. I also spent relatively little time studying for the exams, instead choosing to focus on other classes. For those with less coding experience, your workload may be greater in this aspect.

Labs are super easy, less than 5 min for the first 2 labs, and less than 30 min for the rest.
PAs may take more time. For me, it took like 1-2 hours just to read and fully understand the assignment scenario and what it wants me to do. The actual coding is not too difficult once you understand it, though it may still take quite a bit of time depending on how many tasks are in the PA. The last PA took me a lot of time, maybe like 7+ hours or something like that.

Like I mentioned in the grading section, be careful on the labs and PAs. Aside from hidden test cases, you're allowed to check your score before deadline. As such, you should aim to get 100% on the public test cases. If you're short on time, feel free to use AI to double check your code. Do not use AI to do your assignments, it is not worth it. If, even with ample time, you're unable to do the labs or PAs without AI, you're most likely gonna struggle on the exams since they contain questions that are similar in style to the labs and PAs (though not as long or complicated as the PAs ofc).

comment_content:
The content of the course is comprehensive and truly accelerating in terms of programming skills. It covers all features of control flow, functional programming, recursion (including backtracking and other advanced topics), pointers, dynamic memory allocation, C String features, basics of OOP and basic data structures like linked lists, stacks, queues and BSTs.

Despite the fact that content seems a bit voluminous for beginners, you can get any grade (even solid A or A+) if you read Horstmann, review PPTs, do past papers and solve LeetCode problems in a proper manner and pace.
comment_teaching: I would like to honourably mention my instructor, James Cheung, because he provides a lot of comprehensive materials and stresses extremely non-intuitive edge cases. It was very helpful because, actually, all you have to do to outcompete your peers in midterm and final exams is to complete all short questions without any error. Since they are tricky and meticulously designed, you have to grasp the concepts totally.
comment_workload: Workload is average for CS courses. QA sessions during your labs might be very stressful. Some TAs may urge you to answer the question quickly, some are truly meticulous and may even laugh at you, clicking on your name and “Pass/Half/Fail” table in the sheet. Frankly, they are just joking, but it can be a bit scary (Әйгерім-абла, сәлемәтсіз бе!). Anyway, instructors and TAs are not mean and generally are supportive. Attendance is needed for your labs only, but I would recommend to attend lectures as well.
comment_grading:
Grading schemes seem to be fair. Since the number of students enrolled every semester is high (approximately 500), some rough overall score cutoffs might well be handy. For your reference:

~89-90% A+
~84-85% A
~79-80% A-
…

Please note that such cutoffs are rough and are applicable for the grading scheme:

12% Labs (8x1.5)
24% Assignments (3x8)
24% Midterm (please note that the max score for the midterm is generally not 100, it is lower, so every single error on your midterm affects your overall score dramatically).
40% Final (Anyway, if you are a sigmaboy or gigachad, you may try to farm some kind of comeback aura on your final)

comment_content:
The content is great. C++ is fun. However, at first it might be confusing to people who used to program in Python (like me). In Python, the syntax is very casual. But in C++, it is very strict.

Many people think "pointers" is the hardest topic. I agree, but I think the hardest thing to fully master is "at what circumstances there is an error".

In exams, they can give you a VERY STRANGE CODE (I bet no one will ever code like that) and then ask you whether there is a compilation error / runtime error. I HATE THESE QUESTIONS THE MOST. I will never understand why I need to know whether there is an error when I never code like that. It is like asking a Chinese person about Spanish grammar.
comment_teaching: Cecia teaches very well. Very clear, very engaging.
comment_workload:
8 lab assignments, 3 programming assignments (PAs).

In my opinion, all (except the last programming assignment) were easy and can be finished within hours.

The last programming assignments took me exactly 2 whole days (A whole Saturday and a whole Sunday) to complete. Including debugging, which took me 6-7 hours.

I heard other students complain about the workload of the last programming assignment. The professor replied that typically, 2 days aren't enough for the last assignment.

Since there are hidden testcases, you never know whether your code is really correct, even if you can pass all the shown testcases. Therefore, you really have to be VERY careful when you code.

Some of my friends passed all the shown testcases on the first attempt. But they aren't lucky enough to pass the remaining ones. I, on the other hand, was lucky? Or unlucky? Depending on how you think about it. It's somewhat like 賽翁之馬.

I was unlucky that for PA3, I failed almost all the testcases. And I need to debug case by case (each debugging only solves one or two testcase). And that's why that took me 7 hours. However, after all these debuggings, I was so lucky that I passed all the hidden testcases. I got 100% for all labs and PAs.
comment_grading:
Midterm and Final exam. I got 99% in the midterm and 95.25% in the final. A+ as expected.

The highest score in the midterm and the final was 99% (that was me) and 95.7% (that was my friend). The good thing here is that the marks are divided into many fragments. Even if you are careless about something, you only lose 1 or 2 marks at most. (In contrast, in COMP1021, mistyping one letter can cost you 8 marks --- I experienced it.)

I think I also got the highest overall score in the course. (Yes, I'm showing off)
My overall score is 97.83%, while the highest score shown in Canvas is 97.8%. It looks like Canvas is correcting the high score to 1 decimal place. So I am not the highest if someone got 97.84%.

comment_workload:
Workload is heavy and that's why it is 4 credits
For Spring semester:
1 Quiz, 1 Midterm, 1 Final
8 Labs, 3 PAs
Quiz occurs in 2nd week of semester, and it only covers first few topics

Midterm lasts for 2 hours and final is just a longer midterm with 3 hours including more topics

first few labs takes less than one hour to do but the ones with pointers takes hours to fix memory leak and heap overflow...
remember to attend the labs as they have attendance

PA1 is very easy and should take less than 3 hours
and the rest are PAIN, which lead to the title

PA2 is about "Simplified" Mathematical Expression Evaluator,
Here are the tasks you need to complete in this assignment. In chief, you are required to implement:

A set of recursive math utility functions that are used to calculate the values of mathematical functions, such as square root and power.
A set of recursive string utility functions that are used to manipulate strings, such as a parse_number function that parses a string like "3.14" to a double value 3.14.
An expression evaluator function that evaluates a mathematical expression.
An expression parenthesizer function that adds parentheses and removes extra spaces to/from the expression to make it more readable.
Task 1 and 2 are fine but task 3 and 4 are very painful as you have no idea how to achieve these task with recursions, no loops allowed! I spent my whole weekend and few days around to finish the PA, working 8 hours straight per day, INSANE. I feel my sanity jump mushroom after finishing PA2. The UGTA Harry Li makes this PA, so I will leave a song here for him.
聽我說謝謝你 因為有你 溫暖了四季
謝謝你 感謝有你 世界更美麗
我要謝謝你 因為有你 愛常在Harry
Xie Xie Ni (Thank You) Harry for making my sanity jump mushroom
Initially I want to give Workload a B but considering PA2 it should be C now, following the course title C++
I'm psychologically traumatized!!! (我有心靈創傷啊!!!)
(For your reference, this quote is used by Yahata Umiri (八幡 海鈴) in BanG Dream! Ave Mujica Episode 11, Umiri expressed her traumatization when dialing with Togawa Sakiko due to the absence of Misumi Uika and Togawa Sakiko of their band Ave Mujica training)

And PA3 is slightly better, making Coffee Shop Management System and the pointers are flying everywhere, which is quite messy and I have a hard time on it, still it is less time consuming than PA2. And for PA3 you better get a Linux Virtual environment or Linux Compiler so you can make use of address sanitizer and find memory issues in a easier way. I make use of Github Codespace which is quite useful as codespace terminal g++ is operated in a Linux environment

Usually you will use Visual Studio Code to code your assignments but you may use other editor if you want to, just no online C++ please, you will have a hard time, trust. Also cheating and copying other's code is not allowed. Using GenAI is fine but you must make it does not look so "AI" and pretend you write it yourself. It is not recommended to rely on AI as in the real exam you still need to write them with your bare hands, yes, hand written code on paper.

(Deepseek is better than copilot and GPT in terms of programming imo, still try your best to do it yourself)

PA2 and PA3 could take days or even weeks to complete, start early or else you will have no time to debug and finish them, they take up 24% of grade and need extra attention. Workload A for genius but for noobs like me it should be a C to D I guess, do not trust the AAAA reviews saying that it is 1XXX course or 3 credit workload, this is indeed a 4 CREDITS course and it is indeed a 2000 level course

comment_content:
Refer to other posts for the syllabus. Theres a lot of content to be covered.

Generally, the hardest content before the midterm for me was recursion. After the midterm, really really focus on pointers, and dynamic memory. But honestly, how I view it is:

Before the midterm, all the topics are kind of within their own bubble and can be understood separately. But the content after the midterm is very very inter-related. Things like function overloading aren't standalone topics because they teach you how constructors of classes can be overloaded later on. The entire topic of dynamic memory and pointers has so many other applications as well, etc.

My advice is that before the midterm, you can focus on each topic individually, and you'll be fine just practicing a couple past papers and COMP2011 self tests (on the course website>resources).

However for the topics after the midterm, I think you should really try to see the bigger picture of what you're learning. Especially if u plan on proceeding to 2012.



One of the key tips to understand with cpp is that you really need to understand how things work under the hood. Its not like python where you dont really need to think about how the computer program REALLY interacts with your computer.

For example, in python, you wouldn't ever see a statement like 1+'a', because how does that make sense right? how can you add a to 1? But in cpp we learn how these characters are all binary numbers that have equivalent integer values, so here the character 'a' can be turned into an integer and be added to 1.

Memory allocation, in particular, is key to being able to do well in 2011. In python, you dont ever have to explicitly allocate memory spaces for anything. In fact, we dont even really consider how the program interacts with memory at all. But CPP lacks a lot of the conveniences of languages like python, so u will need to learn DEEPLY how memory works for different variables and data structures.

comment_content: The tutorial sessions are enjoyable and provide a welcoming introduction to programming in C++. The instructor creates a lively and interactive learning environment, which makes the material more approachable. However, the curriculum is somewhat limited in scope, focusing primarily on fundamental concepts such as basic C++ syntax, pointers, classes, and stack operations. While these topics are essential for beginners, the tutorial could benefit from covering more advanced or practical applications to better prepare students for real-world programming challenges. Expanding the content to include topics like dynamic memory management, inheritance, or common data structures would greatly enhance its educational value. Despite this, the tutorial remains a useful starting point for those new to C++
comment_teaching: This course introduces core C++ programming concepts, including pointers, classes, and stack data structures. While lecture notes provide key explanations, students are encouraged to engage in additional self-directed learning to fully master the topics.

However, they’re a great way to deepen your understanding and apply what you’ve learned. You’ll actually enjoy working on them! Plus, the difficulty is manageable since you have unlimited attempts to practice and improve.

comment_content:
Basic C++ programming concepts, variables, control statements, functions, arrays, structs, pointers, dynamic data allocation, linked lists, stacks, queues, binary trees, recursion, and some basic OOP and classes. But more advanced concepts like class inheritances are not included.

comment_content:
Most contents are already there in many other reviews. This is copied from others:

Part 1: Basics and Controls
Part 2: Function (Introduction)
Part 3: Array
Part 4: Recursion

Part 5: Scope
Part 6: Function III:
  - Function declaration
  - Function overloading
  - Default Function Argument

Part 7: Separate Compilation
Part 8: C++ Structure

Part 9: Pointers (Intro)
  - lvalue & rvalue
  - dereference a pointer
  - "const" in pointers initialization
  - Array as (constant) pointers

Part 10: Pointers (Usage)
  - Dynamic allocations (on Heap)
  - Problems on dynamic objects:
     ! Dangling pointer
     ! Memory leak
  - Linked List (LL)
  - LL + Binary Tree
  - Dynamic 1D and 2D array

Part 11: C++ Class (Intro)
  - Class == Struct with its own functions
  - keywords "public" and "private"
  - Constructors
     Trigger when a new object is created
  - Destructor
     Need when the object has pointers
  - Accessor
     Always followed by "const"
     Should NOT modify its data members
  - Mutator
     Opposite to accessors
  - Scope operator
     Syntax: <class_name>::
     Use to separate global function and
     member functions (Class scope)

Part 12: Stack and Queue
  - Stack: Last In First Out (LIFO)
  - Queue: First In First Out (FIFO)
  - Stack & Queue + Class

comment_content:
bg: 2nd yr COMP student, getting A in COMP1021, no more previous programming experience, average IQ???.
friendly reminder:
1.If this course is not needed to fulfil your requirements, I generally don’t recommend taking it unless you achieve A range in COMP1021.
2. If you are a yr1 student and aiming to get into CSE, consider your own situation carefully before enrolling in this course, so that you won’t jeopardize your semester and cga.(I know that some of you are geniuses and can easily handle the stuff)

Now let's go back to the content.
Week 1-3:
Basics, Control Flow. Very basic knowledge about C++ programming, but still lots of details to notice if you only did programming on Python previously.
Week 4-6 (before midterm):
Function (Basics and Recursion), Array. The 2 most difficult topics before the midterm are recursion and array. It’s not easy to think recursively as our brain isn’t designed to think that way. Array have many rules to follow, and it’s very easy to make mistakes like segmentation fault.
Week 7-10:
Scope, Struct, Function (overloading, declaration, default arguments). This part is quite decent compared to others, and it covers some knowledge that you need to know before learning class, pointer.
Week 11-14:
Pointer, Class, Stack & Queue. This is the most challenging part of this course. Lots of concepts and rules are introduced in this part. Personally, I hate the combination of dynamic memory, string and class the most.(Actually I had a similar question about that on the final and messed it up - losing half of the marks on that 18 pts LQ)
comment_teaching:
lecture:
Dimitris’ teaching is decent. He speaks English with a distinct accent, making ‘r’ sound very special, and his tone and talking speed are a bit different from typical English. But I can understand most of that after two or three lectures. Anyway, He is very passionate about teaching and he would like to make interactions with students, fostering a lively classroom environment. After the lectures, he is also friendly to answer questions.

lab:
We have a lab every week except the midterm week, and the lab is actually a half-an-hour tutorial. During the lab, contents that are helpful for programming but not tested in exams like debug and separate compilation are taught by TA. In our section, the TA is Peter. Peter is an interesting guy who likes making political jokes. His teaching is ok but a bit rushed, leaving no time for us to try on our own. In every lecture, Peter uses some memes about our people’s GREAT GREAT Leader Xi (and sometimes Kim) to make us feel engaged, demonstrating that HK is a place embracing free-speech.
comment_workload:
1 lab per week + 3 programming assignments
The workload is even huge compared to other 4-cre courses. Each lab typically took me 2 hours to finish, except the one about recursion which was very annoying to understand the logic. For the PAs, the first two took me 3 days, around 8 hours each to finish. The last one, I spent a week, around 12 hours to finish, including a day purely for debugging (why are there so many memory leaks and segmentation faults…?). Also, since the difficulty of the content, I also spent 1-2 hours after each lecture to revise.
comment_grading:
lab(12%):
Each lab is counted as 3 marks:
1 mark for attendance, recorded before lab session;
1 mark for lab assignment, which is like a homework available 1 week before lab session and due after the lab session;
1 mark for Q&A session, where students are selected to answer a short question about lab assignment.(once every 2 weeks, if the student is not selected, the 1 mark will be counted base on lab assignment)
The total 12% is given according to the percentage of your lab mark.

PA(24%):
Each PA is worth 8%. Before the ddl, about one-third of the test cases are provided for testing, and others are hidden test cases. The PAs are easier compared to previous semesters, with the mean of each being nearly 90.

Exams: hand-written exam with short questions and long questions. In short questions, the format is usually like, providing a program and asking for the output, or filling in the blanks of a piece of code. Every question requires significant effort and can be very annoying. For long questions, the format is like writing a function so that it can work as expected. Since you cannot use a computer, every minor mistake or carelessness during the thinking process will get you into trouble, and you cannot vertify it by running on the computer.

Midterm(24%): mean-37/70 sd-12
The midterm is 2-hr long, testing the content during week 1-6. There are around 10 questions in total, with 6-7 short questions and 3-4 long questions. The most challenging questions of midterm are an SQ about reference variable, a LQ about recursive function, 2 LQ about array.
Final(40%): mean-51/100 sd-23
The final is 3-hr long, testing the content throughout this semester. There are around 10 questions in total, with 6-7 short questions and 3-4 long questions. The most challenging questions of the final are an SQ about scope, pointer, and array, an SQ about loop, a LQ about recursive function, 2 LQ about dynamic array.

B is given to students getting mean of the exams.
A range is 1SD or above.(it seems like I’m near the cutoff of A range, with total 81.5%)
/*update: final grade is A-, I guess I'm slightly above the cutoff, maybe few pts. one of my frds got b+ with 77.5%*/
To pass this course, students need to have total score >= X%, weighted exam score >= Y%, where X is below 50, and Y is below 30.

comment_content:
The course format includes lectures and labs/tutorials. Attendance is not required for lectures but is required for the labs/tutorials. Attendance for the labs is very strict and accounts for large portion of that lab's score. There is one quiz, one midterm, and one final exam. There are a total of around seven labs, but this depends on the time available. Each lab requires a few hours to complete and includes instructions. Also, there are three programming assignments, which may take up to a few days.

The TA's were very helpful. Even if you had difficulties while doing the assignments, they would still give you hints. They aren't allowed to directly help you solve the problems, but their hints are more than enough for you to figure it out yourself.

The syllabus includes the basics of C++ programming (e.g., syntax, the concept of compilation), control, functions, scope, pointers, recursion, stacks, queues, structures, classes and arrays. There are also self-study topics which include file input and output, separate compilation and the string class.

The first half of the course was not too bad. When they get to topic on pointers, stuff gets pretty difficult. But the instructors give plenty of past papers, which I found to be very helpful.

Note: although the materials may seem okay, the problems in the exam are much harder.

Note: although it was said that the self-study topics will not be assessed, they are still somewhat included in the exams.
comment_teaching: This instructor's explanations were very clear. Although our section was relatively slow compared to the others, the pace was just right. The instructor encouraged students to ask questions, which was good. For harder topics such as linked lists, she also made her own slides with her own examples which I found very helpful. She is also very responsive to emails and would give thorough explanations.
comment_workload: For a 4-credit course, the large workload is understandable. The labs were almost weekly. There were no readings required, but the suggested textbook was very helpful.
comment_grading:
The mean was around 60 to 70 for the midterm and final exams. The mean was pretty high for the quiz.

The programming assignments (PAs) and labs contributed a significant part to your final grade. The weighting of the midterm and final exams were pretty similar.
